serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one cup of chilled Thai sago dessert with red tea jelly and coconut milk as typically served in a Thai dessert shop or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Sago dessert with coconut milk — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries for sago (tapioca pearls), coconut milk, mango, sugar, and tea jelly (agar-based)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai red tea jelly sago exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 40% cooked sago pearls (USDA FDC #169717 tapioca, pearl, dry — rehydrated), 30% coconut milk (USDA FDC #169919), 20% mango (USDA FDC #169910), 5% agar jelly (USDA FDC #169276 agar, dried — prepared as jelly), 5% sugar (USDA FDC #169655). Red tea contribution to nutrients is negligible. Values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation at approximately 300 g per serving.

gi_source.title: Tapioca/sago-based dessert GI estimation — analogous to tapioca pudding and coconut milk desserts; GI of tapioca pearls reported as approximately 70 (Atkinson et al., 2008); blended with coconut milk and fruit components lowers effective GI to approximately 65
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai red tea jelly sago. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being cooked sago/tapioca pearls (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). The blending with coconut milk fat (which slows gastric emptying) and the dilution effect of mango and agar jelly components justifies a modest downward adjustment to GI 65 (medium category). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (65 × 18.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 36.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~18 mg per 100 g; ~54 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 54 mg sodium per standard serving, this dessert poses minimal sodium burden for individuals managing hypertension, though portion control remains advisable given its sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~36 g per 300 g serving) driven by refined sago starch and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dessert only occasionally and in small portions, as the high glycemic load from cooked sago pearls and added sugar can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with a protein-rich meal may partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~3.6 g per 100 g; ~10.8 g per serving) and moderate added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from coconut milk (~10.8 g per 300 g serving) exceeds approximately half the recommended daily limit for saturated fat in cardiovascular disease management; individuals with established CVD should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Tea Jelly Sago (ซาโก้วุ้นชาแดง, Sago Wun Cha Daeng) is a chilled Thai dessert combining three primary components: cooked translucent sago pearls suspended in sweetened coconut milk, cubes of firm red tea–flavoured agar jelly, and fresh or lightly sweetened mango pieces. The version sold at Saap Saap Thai in Singapore (marketed as 'VS3 Red Tea Jelly Sago') features a violet-tinted coconut sago base, which derives its colour from butterfly pea flower or food colouring, alongside the red tea jelly and mango. Sago pearls are made from the starch of the sago palm (Metroxylon sagu) or tapioca (cassava starch) and are boiled until translucent and chewy. The coconut milk base is lightly sweetened with sugar and may be infused with pandan. The red tea jelly is prepared by setting brewed red tea (typically Thai red tea or roselle tea) with agar powder, then cutting into cubes. The dessert is served chilled, offering a contrast of textures: soft and chewy sago pearls, firm and slightly bouncy jelly cubes, and juicy mango. The flavour profile is mildly sweet, creamy from the coconut milk, with a subtle floral-astringent note from the red tea jelly. Nutritionally, this dessert is moderate in calories (~112 kcal per 100 g) with the primary macronutrient being carbohydrate (~18.5 g per 100 g), predominantly from sago starch and added sugar. Fat content (~4.2 g per 100 g) is largely saturated fat from coconut milk. Protein is low (~0.8 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), and a standard 300 g serving carries a high glycemic load of approximately 36 g, making it a high-GL food relevant to blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. The dessert is naturally dairy-free, gluten-free, and vegan, and is suitable for halal diets. It is a popular Thai street dessert and restaurant dessert widely available in Thailand and in Thai restaurants across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai red tea jelly sago exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis of USDA FoodData Central entries for the constituent ingredients (sago/tapioca pearls, coconut milk, mango, agar jelly, sugar). GI is an analogous estimate based on tapioca/sago GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central; no direct laboratory analysis or approved regional database entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be treated as indicative. 2. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Beverages' (as in the input seed record) to 'Desserts', as this product is a chilled dessert with solid components (sago pearls, jelly cubes, mango) rather than a beverage. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by component analysis. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as none of the plant-based components (sago, coconut milk, mango, agar, sugar) contribute meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero by component analysis. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as no hydrogenated fats are used in this traditional preparation; confirmed zero by component analysis. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as all ingredients are plant-derived; confirmed zero by component analysis. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from coconut milk fatty acid profile per USDA FDC #169919. 8. glycemic_index set to 65 (medium) as an analogous estimate based on tapioca/sago GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (tapioca pearls GI ~70) adjusted downward for fat dilution from coconut milk. 9. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (65 × 18.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 36.2 g. 10. The 'VS3' prefix in the original food name appears to be a menu item code at Saap Saap Thai restaurant; the canonical food name is 'Red Tea Jelly Sago'. 11. Cuisine tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the input seed record are not applicable to this dessert and have been excluded. 12. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA component sources used for estimation and should not be interpreted as zero.
